items.fix: Split into a stack of smaller PRs (refactor first, then feature, then tests).
items.why: Review quality drops off a cliff above ~400 lines; mega-PRs are rubber-stamped, not reviewed.
items.example: Use `git stack` / `Graphite` / `git-spr` tools, or just open three sequential branches with explicit 'depends on #123' notes.

items.mistake: Empty or one-line PR descriptions ('see title').
items.fix: Fill in What / Why / How / Test plan even for small PRs.
items.why: Reviewers shouldn't have to reverse-engineer intent from the diff; future you using `git blame` will also thank you.
items.example: Even 3 lines is fine: 'Fixes #321. Cache the OpenAI client to avoid re-creating it per request. Test: existing test passes; latency drops 8ms in local benchmark.'

items.mistake: Requesting review while CI is red.
items.fix: Wait for green CI; if blocked on a flaky test, say so explicitly in the PR.
items.why: Pre-CI review wastes the reviewer's time on issues the build was going to flag anyway.
items.example: Use draft PRs while CI is still settling; flip to 'ready for review' only when checks pass.

items.mistake: Treating every reviewer comment as blocking.
items.fix: Reviewers should mark comments as blocking / suggestion / nit; authors should distinguish.
items.why: Without intent signals, authors fix everything (slow) or pick-and-choose silently (frustrating for reviewers).
items.example:
```
[nit] consider renaming `x` to `chunk`
[blocking] this path doesn't handle the empty-list case
[suggestion] extract this into a helper if it's reused
```

items.fix: If you don't have time / context, decline the review and route to someone who does.
items.why: Approving without reading defeats the purpose and trains the team that review is theatre.
items.example: Better: 'Don't have the prompt context to evaluate this; reassigning to @alice.'
